hook: You already know how to check whether a statement is true by trying examples, but examples alone can never prove something works for *all* cases — infinitely many cases demand a smarter strategy. That gap between 'it seems true' and 'it must be true' is exactly what proof techniques are designed to close. By the end of this unit, you'll have a toolkit that lets you tackle almost any 'prove that...' problem with confidence and clarity.
concept: Every proof technique in this unit is a structured strategy for turning logical assumptions into airtight conclusions — together they form the core grammar of mathematical argument, captured by the idea of *valid deductive reasoning from hypotheses to conclusions*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to prove an implication $P \Rightarrow Q$ by assuming $P$ is true and reasoning step-by-step until $Q$ follows inevitably.

Master the technique of proving $P \Rightarrow Q$ indirectly by instead proving its logically equivalent contrapositive $\neg Q \Rightarrow \neg P$, a powerful shortcut when the negation is easier to work with.
Apply the strategy of assuming a statement is false and deriving a logical impossibility, forcing you to conclude the original statement must be true.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Proof by Cases and Without Loss of Generality
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to handle complex statements by splitting them into exhaustive, manageable cases, and to streamline arguments by identifying when one case fully represents all others.
You will learn to rigorously prove both that a mathematical object with a desired property *exists* and, when applicable, that it is the *only* one that does.
Master the art of refuting a universal claim by constructing a single, well-chosen counterexample that makes the statement definitively false.
